description:
For customized expert guidance on your health or term insurance decisions book a FREE chat with us - https://ditto.sh/nihh52
  
Health insurance can be tricky to navigate, especially when you’re deciding whether to port your policy or migrate within the same insurer. 

In this video, we’ll explain the key differences between policy porting and policy migration, helping you understand which option is better suited for your situation. Whether you’re looking for better service, lower premiums, or a new set of features, you’ll find all your answers here.  

What is Policy Migration?  
Migration is when you switch from one health insurance plan to another within the same insurance company. 

What is Policy Porting?  
Porting, on the other hand, involves transferring your health insurance policy from one insurer to another. 

Key Differences Between Porting and Migration:  
1. Timing:  
   - Porting can only be done during the policy renewal period, with advance notice (45-60 days).  
   - Migration is more flexible and can be initiated any time during your policy period.  

2. Documentation:  
   - Porting requires extensive paperwork, including portability forms, KYC documents, claims history, and existing policy details.  
   - Migration is less complicated, as your insurer already has most of your details.  

3. Premiums:  
   - With porting, the new insurer assesses your risk profile and adjusts premiums accordingly.  
   - Migration might result in a slight premium change, depending on the new plan’s features, but generally stays similar.  

When to Choose Porting:  
- You’re dissatisfied with your current insurer’s service.  
- The new insurer offers better claim settlement ratios or a larger hospital network.  
- You’ve found better coverage or lower premiums elsewhere.  

When to Choose Migration:  
- You’re happy with your current insurer but need new features or upgraded coverage.  
- You want a quicker, hassle-free process with minimal documentation.  
- You’re switching from a group policy to an individual policy with the same insurer.  

Key Insights:  
Both options allow you to retain continuity benefits, ensuring your waiting periods and no-claim bonuses are preserved. However, always read the fine print to understand your new policy’s terms.  

Key Takeaways from the Video:  
1. Policy migration is an internal switch within the same insurer; porting moves your policy to a different insurer.  
2. Porting requires advance notice and significant paperwork, while migration is more flexible and less complicated.  
3. Premiums may vary depending on the chosen option and plan.  
4. Both options preserve waiting period continuity and accumulated benefits.  
5. Choose porting for better service or coverage; opt for migration if you want a simpler process within your current insurer.  

Both porting and migration empower you to make informed choices about your health insurance. Whether you’re seeking better service or enhanced features, these options ensure your needs are met. The key is to evaluate your situation and choose wisely.  

Still unsure which option to choose? We’re here to help! Book a FREE chat with our advisors at Ditto to get customized advice on your health insurance -  https://ditto.sh/nihh52

comments.text: In niva bupa migrating from reassured 2.0 to aspire... they are not transfering benefit of no claim bonus. Why does that happening?
comments.author: @sarangduggal3953

comments.text:
As per IRDAI guidelines on porting and migration, the bonus amount itself isn’t carried forward. However, if you’re applying for a higher base sum insured than your current one, the waiting period on the increased amount can be waived off using your existing bonus.
In migration cases, it’s largely up to the insurance company and their underwriting team to decide whether or not to carry forward the bonus amount, since there’s no fixed rule mandating it.

comments.text: Sir, I have a question. I have a friend whose leg was cut off. He needs to undergo a leg transplant operation. Can this be covered under health insurance?
comments.author: @AnshuSingh-er9fn

comments.text:
leg transplant operation. Can this be covered under health insurance?
Case 1: Person is already covered under a health insurance plan
If the leg loss was due to an accident, the hospitalisation and surgery (including transplant, if medically advised) may be covered, depending on the policy.

Prosthetic limbs are usually not covered unless clearly included or added through an optional rider.

All claims will need proper documentation and medical justification.

Case 2: Person is planning to buy health insurance now
If the condition already exists, it will likely be excluded or have a long waiting period (typically 2–3 years).

For something as major as a leg transplant, new insurance will not cover it immediately and might permanently exclude it after underwriting. 
Outright rejection might also happen.
However, applying for a plan is still wise for future needs and protection.

To understand what’s possible in your friend’s case and which plans could be suitable, we’d recommend getting in touch with our advisors via chat or call — they'll guide you with the exact details based on the profile - https://ditto.sh/nihh52

comments.text: Can I migrate on renewal if I claimed twice in last year after 5 years,
comments.author: @kalyanmishra6650

comments.text:
Hi! Migration — which means switching to another plan within the same insurance company — is possible at renewal, but it depends on several factors. These include the specific policy you currently have, the insurer’s internal guidelines, the age of the insured members, and any pre-existing medical conditions.

Claiming twice in the last year generally isn’t a problem. However, if one of the claims was for a critical illness (cancer, heart attacks), some insurers may be cautious and might not approve the migration, depending on their underwriting rules.

If migration doesn’t go through, you can consider porting your policy to a different insurer. But just like migration, the issuance of the new policy will be subject to underwriting and approval by the new insurer.
Please note that the window to initiate migration or porting is typically 45–60 days before your policy renewal, so it’s best to start the process within that timeframe.
For more clarity based on your exact situation, feel free to book a call or chat with one of our advisors — they’ll help you explore the best possible options for your needs - https://ditto.sh/nihh52
comments.author: @dittoinsurance

comments.text: Will moratorium period reset in case of migration ? ( The period was 8 year earlier and now 5 year after recent IRDA change in 2024). In case of porting, it would definitely change, but what about migration within the same insurance company ?
comments.author: @mr1ansh

comments.text:
No, the moratorium period will be carried forward in both migration and porting cases. As per the latest IRDAI guidelines (2024), the revised moratorium period is 5 years (earlier 8 years), and this updated duration applies across both scenarios without resetting the clock. That said, we still recommend honest disclosure of all past and current health conditions to avoid any issues during claims.

For more detailed information, you can refer to the IRDAI Master Circular here: https://irdai.gov.in/documents/37343/365525/%25e0%25a4%25b8%25e0%25a5%258d%25e0%25a4%25b5%25e0%25a4%25be%25e0%25a4%25b8%25e0%25a5%258d%25e0%25a4%25a5%25e0%25a5%258d%25e0%25a4%25af+%25e0%25a4%25ac%25e0%25a5%2580%25e0%25a4%25ae%25e0%25a4%25be+%25e0%25a4%25b5%25e0%25a5%258d%25e0%25a4%25af%25e0%25a4%25b5%25e0%25a4%25b8%25e0%25a4%25be%25e0%25a4%25af+%25e0%25a4%25aa%25e0%25a4%25b0+%25e0%25a4%25ae%25e0%25a4%25be%25e0%25a4%25b8%25e0%25a5%258d%25e0%25a4%259f%25e0%25a4%25b0+%25e0%25a4%25aa%25e0%25a4%25b0%25e0%25a4%25bf%25e0%25a4%25aa%25e0%25a4%25a4%25e0%25a5%258d%25e0%25a4%25b0+_+Master+Circular++on+Health++Insurance+Business++29052024.pdf/5e707a91-b5de-1ec1-cf18-b66273a6839d?version=1.0&t=1716962621002
